ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Nasi Kuning; 'rice dish' (FOODON_03311069) is the closest defensible broader class. A more specific term for turmeric coconut rice is absent from the ontology as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Nasi Kuning. A000E (Rice-based dishes) is the closest defensible broader category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to Nasi Kuning as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT clinical coding is not applicable for this food record.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Nasi Kuning is approximately 250 g (one plate), providing around 420 kcal, 65 g carbohydrate, 15 g fat, and 7.5 g protein.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nasi Kuning is not listed as a discrete entry in the HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore database entry for coconut rice (Nasi Lemak rice base) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked white rice (ID 168880) adjusted for coconut milk addition and turmeric. The input values (energy 168 kcal, protein 3.0 g, fat 6.0 g, carbohydrate 26.0 g per 100 g) are consistent with this adaptation and are retained. Saturated fat 4.0 g/100 g is consistent with coconut milk contribution. Trans fat 0.04 g/100 g is a trace level consistent with coconut-derived fat. Cholesterol 0.0 mg/100 g is confirmed as this is a plant-based dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — White rice, boiled (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nasi Kuning in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 64 is estimated from the closest analogous food: white rice, boiled (GI ~64–72, median 64 used), as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Coconut milk addition may marginally lower GI due to fat content; however, no direct study is available, so the conservative white-rice GI of 64 is applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (64 × 65.0) / 100 = 41.6. Carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 26.0 × 2.5 = 65.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 140 mg/100 g — below the 200 mg/100 g threshold; plant-based dish with no added animal fats
hypertension.guidance: Nasi Kuning is suitable for individuals with hypertension when consumed in moderate portions; however, sodium from accompanying side dishes and condiments (e.g., sambal, salted fish) should be monitored carefully, as these can substantially increase total meal sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ~41.6) driven by white rice base with low dietary fibre (0.8 g/100 g); GI estimated at 64 (medium-high for a refined carbohydrate staple)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size of Nasi Kuning to no more than 100–150 g per meal, pair it with high-fibre and high-protein side dishes such as tempeh, tofu, or leafy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response, and consider substituting brown rice or cauliflower rice as a lower-GI base.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.0 g/100 g from coconut milk; a 250 g serving delivers 10 g saturated fat, approaching the daily limit for individuals with CVD
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Nasi Kuning to small portions (100–150 g) due to the saturated fat contribution from coconut milk; turmeric (curcumin) provides anti-inflammatory benefit, but this does not offset the saturated fat load at full serving sizes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Turmeric (curcumin) is the defining ingredient and provides well-evidenced anti-inflammatory activity; saturated fat 4.0 g/100 g is below the 5 g/100 g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Nasi Kuning is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory properties of turmeric; portion size should be moderated to limit saturated fat intake from coconut milk, and the dish should be accompanied by anti-inflammatory sides such as tempeh or leafy greens.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Kuning (Indonesian/Malay: 'yellow rice'; Chinese: 黄姜饭) is a fragrant Southeast Asian rice dish cooked by steaming or simmering white rice in coconut milk together with turmeric, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, and pandan leaves, which give the rice its characteristic vibrant yellow colour and layered aromatic profile. The dish originates from Indonesia and is widely consumed across the Malay Archipelago and in Singapore, where it is a staple at Malay and Indonesian food stalls. The texture is slightly richer and more cohesive than plain steamed rice due to the coconut milk, and the flavour is mildly savoury with earthy, floral, and citrus-herbal notes from the spice blend. Nasi Kuning is traditionally served as a centrepiece rice at festive occasions and everyday meals alike, accompanied by a variety of side dishes including fried chicken, tempeh, tofu, sambal, boiled eggs, and vegetables. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 168 kcal, 3.0 g protein, 6.0 g fat (of which 4.0 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), and 26.0 g carbohydrate with only 0.8 g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is estimated at 64 (medium), yielding a high glycemic load of approximately 41.6 per standard 250 g serving, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is naturally halal, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Turmeric contributes curcumin, a bioactive compound with anti-inflammatory properties relevant to conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. Sodium at 140 mg/100 g is moderate and within acceptable limits for hypertension management when side dishes are chosen carefully.
common_user_queries: How many calories in nasi kuning?
Is nasi kuning suitable for diabetics?
What is yellow rice made of?
Is nasi kuning high in carbs?
What is the difference between nasi kuning and nasi lemak?

confidence_reason: Nasi Kuning is not listed as a discrete entry in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest approved-source analogues (HPB coconut rice / USDA cooked white rice adjusted for coconut milk). The input values from the GrabFood-sourced record are consistent with this adaptation and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from white rice data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from HPB Singapore coconut rice analogue and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked white rice (ID 168880) adjusted for coconut milk addition; no exact Nasi Kuning entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for Nasi Kuning as a composite dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024; these fields should be updated when a direct laboratory analysis or approved-source composite entry becomes available. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): Nasi Kuning is a plant-based dish containing no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally zero. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): white rice and coconut milk contain negligible vitamin C; trace amounts from turmeric and aromatics are below detection threshold in cooked form. 5. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate from white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for Nasi Kuning exists in any approved GI source. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.8) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.2) are estimated from the fatty acid profile of coconut milk and white rice per USDA SR Legacy; these are approximate and should be confirmed by direct analysis. 7. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) has been corrected to the canonical HPB Foodo URL (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/). 8. Tamil and Hindi local names for Nasi Kuning are not in common use; these fields are left null as no established Tamil or Hindi name exists for this Indonesian/Malay dish. 9. The input listed country_of_origin as Singapore; this has been corrected to Indonesia / Southeast Asia as Nasi Kuning originates from Indonesia, though it is widely consumed in Singapore.
